old_claim.note: Military Waystations provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim.note: Vindolanda accounts record military-unit administration, supplies, food, clothing, stabling, wagon components, and travel expenses, making logistics the relevant operational context.

source_shape.source_locator: The Accounts and lists introduction notes records for food, drink, clothing, stabling, carriage axles, wagon components, beer, boot nails, salt, wheat, and meat.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The Vindolanda records track supply, transport, and commodity administration around an army fort.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: Logistics_supply_lines is the broader operational setting documented by the accounts; Achaemenid military_waystations were a different road-station institution and should not carry the Roman record scope.

ontology_before: Military Depot Records depended on Achaemenid military waystations, conflating Roman frontier administrative records with a separate Persian road-station node.
ontology_after: Military Depot Records keeps a logistics context through logistics_supply_lines, while the Achaemenid waystation node remains scoped to royal-road stations and couriers.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed: military_depot_records now depends on logistics_supply_lines and no longer directly depends on military_waystations.
would_reject_if: The node is later narrowed to records made at road waystations rather than fort administration.
A source shows the target records were unrelated to supply, transport, commodities, or unit logistics.
A narrower Roman quartermaster logistics node is added and should replace broad logistics_supply_lines.
short_reason: Roman military records fit logistics administration, not the Achaemenid waystation node.
